_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Scotland County, NC. Created from Richmond County in 1899 — records begin at that date. Earlier records for Scotland County families are in Richmond County. Lumbee families in Scotland County face the same three-race classification challenges as in Robeson County.
display_name: Scotland County, North Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

current_courthouse.location: Laurinburg, NC — Main Street (original courthouse, expanded and renovated multiple times)

previous_courthouses.fate: Scotland County was part of Richmond County before 1899. All records before that date are filed in Richmond County. The Richmond County Courthouse in Rockingham is 30 miles north of Laurinburg — researchers tracing pre-1899 families must check Richmond County records.

record_losses.cause: None — Scotland County has experienced no major courthouse fire or catastrophic record loss
records_survived: All major record series from 1899 onward
record_losses.description: Scotland County's records are intact from the county's formation in 1899. The challenge is not missing records but the 1899 boundary — families living in what became Scotland County before 1899 generated records that are filed in Richmond County, not Scotland County.
record_losses.narrative_impact: A genealogist searching Scotland County records will find nothing before 1899 — not because the records are lost, but because the county did not exist. {ancestor_name}'s earlier records are in Richmond County, thirty miles north in Rockingham.
record_losses.source: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records. Raleigh, 2001.

deeds.completeness: Complete from county formation. Pre-1899 deeds for this area are in Richmond County.
deeds.gaps: None in Scotland County records. The 1899 gap is a jurisdiction boundary, not a loss.
deeds.repository: Scotland County Register of Deeds, Laurinburg; NC State Archives microfilm

wills_estates.gaps: Pre-1899 estates for this area are in Richmond County
wills_estates.repository: Scotland County Clerk of Court; NC State Archives

marriages.completeness: Complete from county formation. Three-race filing system (white, Indian, colored) mirrors Robeson County after 1899.
marriages.gaps: Pre-1899 marriages in Richmond County records
marriages.repository: Scotland County Register of Deeds; NC State Archives microfilm

court_minutes.gaps: Pre-1899 court proceedings in Richmond County
court_minutes.repository: Scotland County Clerk of Court; NC State Archives

vital_records.completeness: Spotty until approximately 1920, as in all NC counties

substitute_sources.description: All civil records for Scotland County families before 1899 — deeds, marriages, wills, court minutes — are filed in Richmond County at the courthouse in Rockingham, NC.
substitute_sources.repository: Richmond County Courthouse, Rockingham, NC; NC State Archives microfilm
substitute_sources.era_coverage: 1779-1899 (Richmond County formation to Scotland County formation)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracing any Scotland County family before 1899. Researchers must check Richmond County records — there is no Scotland County substitute for this period.
substitute_sources.source: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records. Raleigh, 2001.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790-1940. Before 1900, Scotland County families were enumerated in Richmond County. The same racial classification instability applies to Lumbee families here as in Robeson County.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Trace families from Richmond County census (pre-1900) to Scotland County census (1900+). Watch for racial reclassification across the transition.

substitute_sources.source_type: Church records — Burnt Swamp Baptist Association
substitute_sources.description: Lumbee communities in Scotland County were served by Burnt Swamp Baptist Association churches that spanned the Robeson-Scotland county line. The same church records that document Robeson County Lumbee families document Scotland County Lumbee families.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual congregations; Baptist Historical Collection, Wake Forest University
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for Scotland County Lumbee families, as in Robeson County. Church membership confirms identity and documents family connections.
substitute_sources.source: Lowery, Lumbee Indians in the Jim Crow South (2010).

narrative_hooks: Scotland County did not exist before 1899 — the courthouse in Laurinburg has no records older than {ancestor_name}'s grandparents. To trace the family further back, the trail leads to the Richmond County courthouse in Rockingham, thirty miles north.

If {ancestor_name} was born in what became Scotland County before 1899, there is no Scotland County birth record to find — the family was still in Richmond County when the baby arrived, and the records are filed under the old county name.

{ancestor_name}'s marriage in Scotland County after 1899 was filed in one of three racial categories — white, Indian, or colored — and which file it ended up in depended on how the clerk classified the couple, not on how the family identified themselves.

The church records from the Burnt Swamp Association do not respect the county line between Robeson and Scotland — the same congregation that served {ancestor_name}'s family spread across both counties, and the membership rolls are filed by church, not by jurisdiction.
source_references: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.
Lowery, Malinda Maynor. Lumbee Indians in the Jim Crow South. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 2010.
Lefler, H.T., and A.R. Newsome. North Carolina: The History of a Southern State. 3rd ed. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1973.
